analysis: Temporal comparison of H across transmission regimes

notes: Papyrus P66 (~200 CE, Gospel of John) is not available as a digital morphologically-tagged corpus in standard NLP repositories (MorphGNT, Perseus, AGDT). The papyrus text exists in transcription but without POS tagging and verse-level word counts needed for H analysis. A second temporal point for NT transmission is therefore not available.

temporal_points.source: Quranic Arabic Corpus v0.4 (based on Uthmanic tradition)

notes: The Samarkand Codex (~700 CE) is not available as a digital morphologically-tagged corpus. Only one temporal point is available for the Quran.

temporal_points.source: AGDT (Perseus Project, based on modern critical editions)

notes: Only one temporal point available. Composition ~750 BCE, oral transmission ~750-550 BCE, first written versions ~550 BCE. Current text reflects ~2,800 years of transmission, mostly uncontrolled after the Alexandrian period (~200 BCE).

temporal_points.source: AGDT (Perseus Project, based on modern critical editions)

notes: Only one temporal point available. Composition ~440 BCE, manuscript tradition through Byzantium. Current text reflects ~2,400 years of free (scribal) transmission.

temporal_points.source: Digital Corpus of Sanskrit (DCS), first manuscripts ~1000 CE

notes: Composition ~1500-1200 BCE, oral transmission continuously controlled. Only one temporal point (modern critical text). No pre-manuscript corpus available.

temporal_points.source: Sefaria API (based on Vilna edition, ~1880 CE)

notes: Composition ~200 CE, redacted by Rabbi Judah ha-Nasi. Transmission semi-controlled (rabbinic academies). Only one temporal point available.

limitation: Only one corpus (AT/Isaiah) has two measured temporal points (DSS ~100 BCE and WLC ~1000 CE). For all others, only upper-bound estimates are available. P66 (Papyrus, NT ~200 CE) is not available as a digital morphologically-tagged corpus.
